content:
If you've been writing C# for more than a few years, you've written thousands of DTOs. Request models, response models, event payloads, integration contracts — the connective tissue of every distributed system. And if you're like most of us, you've written nearly all of them as classes with { get; set; } properties, because that's what we've done since .NET Framework 2.0.

Here's the uncomfortable truth: a mutable class is the wrong data structure for a DTO, and it has been since C# 9 gave us a better one. Not "stylistically wrong." Wrong in ways that show up as production incidents — duplicate payments, corrupted audit trails, and race conditions in async pipelines.

This article makes the case from first principles, with examples drawn from banking and fintech systems — payment processing, reconciliation, maker-checker workflows — because that's where the cost of getting this wrong is measured in money, not just bugs. Whether you're a junior engineer writing your first API contract or a solutions architect reviewing a payment platform design, the goal is the same: understand why records exist, not just how to type them.

🚚 What Are DTOs in Practice?

Imagine you're building a ride-sharing app like Uber or Bolt. When the frontend requests a list of nearby drivers, you return a DriverDto:

{
"id": 142,
"name": "Linda",
"rating": 4.9
}

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

In C#, you might write:

public class DriverDto
{
public int Id { get; set; }
public string Name { get; set; }
public double Rating { get; set; }
}

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

This works — but it's bloated, mutable, and doesn't reflect the true intent of a DTO: a simple, structured data carrier.

Now zoom out from ride-sharing to where DTOs really earn their keep: system boundaries in enterprise software. In a payments platform, DTOs are everywhere:

• The inbound webhook payload from a card scheme or a partner bank notifying you of a settlement.

• The API response your mobile app receives when a customer checks their balance.

• The Kafka event published when a transfer moves from PENDING to SETTLED.

• The reconciliation record you compare against the processor's end-of-day file.

Every one of these is a contract. It represents a fact — "this transaction, with this reference, for this amount, at this time" — communicated across a boundary. And here is the key insight the whole article rests on:

A DTO is a snapshot of data at a point in time. Snapshots don't change. So why are we modeling them with a type whose defining feature is that it changes?

A junior engineer can think of it this way: a DTO is like a printed bank statement. Once it's printed, nobody should be able to grab a pen and change the numbers. If the numbers need to be different, you print a new statement. Classes with set; accessors hand everyone a pen. Records take the pen away.

🔍 The Real Problems with Class-Based DTOs

Let's be precise about what's wrong, because "bloated" alone wouldn't justify changing a decade of habit. There are four concrete failures.

1. Mutability is a liability at boundaries

Consider a simplified inbound payment notification in a fintech platform:

public class PaymentNotificationDto
{
public string TransactionRef { get; set; }
public decimal Amount { get; set; }
public string Currency { get; set; }
public string SenderAccount { get; set; }
public DateTime ReceivedAt { get; set; }
}

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

This object will pass through validation, enrichment, fraud screening, ledger posting, and notification services — often across async hops and sometimes across threads. Any one of those stages can silently mutate it. A well-meaning teammate writes:

// "Normalizing" inside the fraud check — seems harmless
notification.Amount = Math.Round(notification.Amount, 2);

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

Now the amount that reaches your ledger is not the amount you received from the partner bank. When reconciliation runs at end of day and finds a ₦0.005 discrepancy across two million transactions, you'll spend a very unpleasant week finding this line. The compiler could have prevented it. With a mutable class, it can't.

In banking, this isn't hypothetical. The single most valuable property of financial data in flight is that it cannot be tampered with between the boundary and the ledger. Mutability is an open invitation to tampering — usually accidental, occasionally worse.

2. Reference equality is the wrong equality

Two DTOs carrying identical data are, for every practical purpose, the same fact. But classes compare by reference:

var a = new PaymentNotificationDto { TransactionRef = "TXN-8842", Amount = 50_000m, Currency = "NGN" };
var b = new PaymentNotificationDto { TransactionRef = "TXN-8842", Amount = 50_000m, Currency = "NGN" };
Console.WriteLine(a == b); // false
Console.WriteLine(a.Equals(b)); // false

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

Why does this matter? Idempotency — the backbone of every payment system. Partner banks retry webhooks. Kafka delivers at-least-once. Mobile apps resubmit on timeout. Your system must recognize "I've seen this exact fact before" cheaply and correctly. With classes, you either hand-write Equals/GetHashCode (and forget to update them when a property is added — a classic source of subtle dedup bugs), pull in a library, or compare serialized JSON like a barbarian.

3. The boilerplate hides the contract

A class-based DTO with proper equality, immutability via constructor injection, and a useful ToString for structured logging is 40–60 lines. The contract — the thing an engineer actually needs to read — is 5 lines. The other 90% is ceremony that must be maintained, reviewed, and kept in sync. In an enterprise codebase with 400 DTOs, that's tens of thousands of lines of pure noise, every line a place for a bug to hide.

4. Nothing communicates intent

public class PaymentNotificationDto tells a reader nothing about how the type is meant to be used. Is it safe to cache? Safe to share across threads? Is mutation expected? The type system is silent, so the answer lives in tribal knowledge and code review vigilance — the two most expensive and least reliable enforcement mechanisms in software.

✨ Enter Records: The Type That Says What It Means

The same DTO as a record:

public record PaymentNotificationDto(
string TransactionRef,
decimal Amount,
string Currency,
string SenderAccount,
DateTime ReceivedAt);

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

Five lines. And the compiler generates, for free:

•
Init-only properties — the object is frozen after construction.

•
Value-based Equals and GetHashCode — two records with the same data are equal.

•
A readable ToString — PaymentNotificationDto { TransactionRef = TXN-8842, Amount = 50000, ... }, which is a quiet superpower for structured logging in Serilog.

•
Deconstruction — var (txnRef, amount, currency, _, _) = dto;

•
with expressions — non-destructive "mutation" that produces a new copy.

Our idempotency check now just works:

var a = new PaymentNotificationDto("TXN-8842", 50_000m, "NGN", "0123456789", timestamp);
var b = new PaymentNotificationDto("TXN-8842", 50_000m, "NGN", "0123456789", timestamp);
Console.WriteLine(a == b); // true — same fact, recognized as such

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

And the tampering bug from earlier? It's now a compile-time error:

notification.Amount = Math.Round(notification.Amount, 2);
// CS8852: Init-only property can only be assigned in an object initializer

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

The pen has been taken away. If a stage in your pipeline genuinely needs a normalized amount, it must be explicit about creating a new fact:

var normalized = notification with { Amount = Math.Round(notification.Amount, 2) };

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

Notice what happened to the semantics: mutation-in-place (invisible, untraceable) became transformation (visible, reviewable, loggable). In a regulated environment where auditors ask "show me every place this value could have changed," that difference is not cosmetic.

🏦 Records in the Wild: Enterprise Fintech Scenarios

Abstract arguments are cheap. Let's walk through four scenarios that recur in nearly every banking or fintech platform and see how records change the engineering.

Scenario 1: Idempotent webhook ingestion

Your platform receives settlement notifications from multiple partner banks. Networks are unreliable; partners retry aggressively. A duplicate posting means a customer gets credited twice — a direct financial loss.

public record SettlementNotification(
string ProviderRef,
string SessionId,
decimal Amount,
string Currency,
string BeneficiaryAccount,
DateTimeOffset SettledAt);

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

Because records give you correct GetHashCode for free, deduplication within a processing window becomes trivially correct:

// In-memory dedup for a processing window — HashSet just works
private readonly HashSet<SettlementNotification> _seenInWindow = new();
public async Task HandleAsync(SettlementNotification notification)
{
if (!_seenInWindow.Add(notification))
{
_logger.LogWarning("Duplicate settlement ignored: {Notification}", notification);
return; // structured log includes full payload via generated ToString
}
await _ledger.PostAsync(notification);
}

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

With a class, HashSet.Add would happily accept ten copies of the same notification, because each is a distinct reference. You'd need hand-rolled equality — and every hand-rolled GetHashCode is a future bug when someone adds NarrationText to the DTO and forgets to update the hash.

(In production you'd also have a durable idempotency store keyed on ProviderRef + SessionId — value equality complements that; it doesn't replace it. More on this in the limitations section.)

Scenario 2: Event-driven architecture on Kafka

In an event-driven core banking system, events are facts about the past: TransferInitiated, FraudCheckPassed, TransferSettled. The first rule of event sourcing is that facts are immutable — you never rewrite history; you append new events.

public record TransferInitiated(
Guid TransferId,
string SourceAccount,
string DestinationAccount,
decimal Amount,
string Currency,
DateTimeOffset InitiatedAt) : IDomainEvent;

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

When a consumer replays this event during recovery or a new projection is built from the event log, the guarantee that no handler anywhere could have mutated the event is what makes replay deterministic. With mutable class events, one handler that "fixes up" a field poisons every downstream consumer in the same process — and you'll only discover it when a rebuilt projection disagrees with the ledger.

Records make the architectural rule ("events are immutable facts") a compiler-enforced invariant instead of a wiki page nobody reads.

Scenario 3: Maker-checker workflows

Regulated back-office operations — bulk account onboarding, limit changes, manual reversals — follow maker-checker: one officer initiates, another approves. The state transition must be explicit and auditable.

public record ReversalRequest(
Guid RequestId,
string TransactionRef,
decimal Amount,
string MakerId,
string? CheckerId,
ApprovalStatus Status,
DateTimeOffset CreatedAt,
DateTimeOffset? DecidedAt);

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

The with expression models the transition beautifully — the original request is preserved untouched, and approval produces a new state:

public ReversalRequest Approve(ReversalRequest request, string checkerId) =>
request with
{
CheckerId = checkerId,
Status = ApprovalStatus.Approved,
DecidedAt = _clock.UtcNow
};

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

For audit purposes you can log both objects — before and after — and the generated ToString gives the auditor a complete, readable diff. Try producing that with a
